content:
​Matatu-mates, to us guys are a life goal. The pretty girls we meet in matatus are the second reason we use perfume. (the first being we probably didn't shower that day) Not that we engage them in conversations anyway. No. We just love that feeling of sitting next to those beautiful girls we only interact with on social media.  That lightskin who posted on Facebook about her instagram account "Follow for follow". You rush to insta and find she follows a mere 200 out of her 15K followers. Either the school you attended got the meaning of the second "follow" wrong or your eyes are messing. Certainly not that this damsel is lying. Is she? No. Hell no! So you just follow her and wait to be "followed back". Does she? Of course she does. Why wouldn't she? You are, after all the son of Mzee Murage who sits at the council of kiama in every ruracio in the village and you boast 321 followers out of a folowing of 1000+. So, yes she will follow you back. But now you're together in a mat.(what is sako kwa bako in English btw?) Now she will notice you by fire by force. She has to know even you "Nairobi you arrived." She needs to know you also use that large infinix she ignores your DMs with-only you don't have the pink cover. She must know that when she pulls out fifty bob from her huge handbag to pay the fare, you shall take it, put it in your pockets and pay for wawili using a one hundred shilling note because your father also sold a cow so that you go to school and learn maths. You shall act like nothing happened because you're awesome like that. You shall fake a call and speak fluent English because your teasher was Mr. Kimani with the motorbike.  You will fake an argument with an imaginary "client" and act cool about it because that is the kind of man she and her Guarana-addict friends have as "goals". You shall ignore the tattoo on her left thigh. The one of an eagle with a bleeding eye, broken talons, rugged feather, her initials GMA written in italicized bold and whose artist probably shook because the wings are slightly assymetrical...okay yeah. You get the drift. DON'T ignore that one.  Generally, there's always that feeling that you have "arrived" when you enter a mat and find an empty seat next to a girl with real eyebrows. With strong, classy perfume reminding you that there's hope for Nairobi, global warming notwithstanding. It is always a good sign. Don't argue with science. And if it so happens that she skips all seats and voluntarily chooses the one next to you-especially if the bus is empty-my friend, it's time to go home. You've had too many a bottle. These things don't happen.  So on this particular day I dared to dream. I have already established Thursdays to be my lucky day. Back since my high school days. I mean, wasn't it the day when we would finally withdraw our money from the bursar's office at school? Were we not usually served sausages in the morning for breakfast? Wasn't supper normally either fish or chicken? Not to mention it was the day I left school for good.  I wasn't taking any more chances with my Thursdays. The other Thursday I'd accidentally met my crush (pronounced girlfriend) in town wearing the same shirt she'd seen me in for three consecutive times. Why do these things happen though? So that day I was going to meet her, in a different shirt. A white one. A plain white shirt in case you didn't hear the first time. I had showered 90% for her, 9% because it is the right thing to do and -99% because it was January and there was no use wasting water trying to look clean for people. So yes, that maths adds up to I showered 0%. I sat there oozing with the masculine scent of my designer perfume plastered on layers of dirt. I patiently waited for maiden in glowing gown to waltz into the seat next to mine. We would then have a quiet, pleasant drive to wherever I was going. This would later be narrated to my boys as how I swept a stranger girl of her  matatu seat using my smooth vibes. So much that she refused to give me her number to "protect her relationship." I had a pair of white shorts. Again, I say, plain white shorts. I had bought Delamere Vanilla yoghurt like I always do when travelling. Not cool, I know. But if I were to choose between Vanilla yoghurt and your validation of my coolness, I'd ask you to rate my hotness on a scale of you to me. (Pronounced 0-10) Either my Thursday fàiry had missed the bus or today wasn't precisely a Thursday in the fairy calendar. There's this lady who entered the bus, carrying like four large bags with a baby tugging at her jeans screaming, "Mummy nataka ileee..." Right after her, enters, ladies and gentlemen, my preferred matatu-mate for the day. The lady with the still screaming baby struggles into the bus and places her bags onto the seat directly opposite me. Phew! Not that I have a problem with sitting next to mothers with small babies. I mean, who doesn't like making faces at them and acting all serious when they start crying. Like we have no idea why. I just preferred instagram-beauty league infinix user at that moment. On the real though, she had an infinix. That large one with a fingerprint sensor beneath the camera.   What happened next happened so fast, I still try to  discover how I had no time to say "nimeshikia mtu hapa." I was busy texting Bae that nimefika nyayo, jam imeshikana. I'm coming. Never mind I'd just boarded a manyanga at Galleria. The baby pointed at me. Well, at my yoghurt but no, I didn't see that. It was January and not even innocent baby eyes were going to convince me to part with the product of my hard-earned 100bob. Pesa otas my friend. "Unataka kukaa hapo?" Silence. "Utakaa hapo mummy?" Silence Sn 2. "Sawa basi, tukae hapa na uncle, sindio kamum?"  Wait, I'm sorry, what now??!! In what language does two silences make a yes again? I tried making faces at the baby to coerce her to change her mother's mind but if only my face had been on that yoghurt pack.  Mother placed one bag down, sat baby on her lap and squeezed one of the plastic bags between us. I mean, she just sat. No slow motion, no sad background music, no apologies for killing a dream. She just sat.  I offered to help with the baby as the engine roared to life. She had her hands full,for one, and most importantly, there was absolutely no way I was placing those bags anywhere near my all white clad.  "Enda ushikwe na uncle" Oh lord no! "Na uncle?" "Eeh, na uncle" If you think finding the perfect balance between the taste of ugali and sukuma wiki in your mouth is hard,then you definitely haven't tried visually communicating to a three-year-old Nairobi girl you just met that your relationship is still too young to be sharing yoghurt already. "Uncle nataka ii," she said, pointing at my yoghurt. My friend if you have not mastered the art of biting lemon by the time you're leaving your village for the city, don't come.  See why I don't like titles? The way I heard iñ Uganda once you acquire the title "husband" you automatically owe someone's daughter a house. Now this young girl thinks I owe her my yoghurt because I'm her de facto "uncle." Poor girl also probably thinks I'd reserved the seat her mum is on for them. Lol! She has no idea. The little girl, now my apparent niece (I'll be having a word with my brother over this issue. I wouldn't be surprised if I was actually the uncle though) threw a mini tantrum over my yoghurt as her mother sat there, in silence, scrolling through her Facebook feed. I mean, people!! What happened to some good ol' Whooping oñ the behind. I remember at, almost that age, my mother gave me a thrashing at the supermarket tills. True story!
See there's this Asian boy who had picked a "jawbreaker" from the shelves. He unwrapped it and stuck the huge sweet in his mouth. He then took the wrapper to the till where her mum was and she paid for it. #ThugLife, right? After careful observation, this seemed like a cool enough idea to try at my age. So I'm here busy trying to unwrap jawbreaker. Small small, I catch the scent of my mum's perfume and it finally hits me we don't share a mum with the Asian boy who, by now, is in the car being praised for being responsible. I had no time to react. My cheeks were in between her index fingers and thumbs on both sides. She pulled me so hard I was almost suspended in the air. Then out of nowhere the release of my cheeks elastically catapulted two rap tap slaps straight in my face. Those days nobody used to tetea you when you were being beaten. No. People would go on shopping as if a baby sprawling on the floor screaming for help from its own mother was a shopping experience the supermarket owed them.  The only thing that was getting me out of this situation was if I climbed the Nakumatt elephant and rode it all the way back to Thailand. In retrospect, I can't exactly tell how that beating added any value to my life but I would sure as hell do the same to my daughter. Right after naming her after my mother,  of course and screaming to her,"Susan ni aibu gani unaniletea mbele ya watu, eeh?" Sweet revenge, right? Now that I think of it, who was bringing who shame?
I tightly shut the lid of my yoghurt and gave it to her. Knock yourself out! This is Kenya my friend. Things don't come easy. Four unsuccessful times later, she hurled the tetra  paçk at me, missing the danger zone by inches. At this, her mother took her from me, fearing-rightfully so-that I was about to toss the little bundle of misery off the window, after she had earlier, also deliberately stepped on the hem of my shorts.MY.WHITE.SHORTS!! She pinched me and slapped me on the lap for the third time in a row as her mother smiled at me sheepishly. Then she finally broke the silence, "Huyu akiwa na njaa anakuanga hivi. Wacha kupiga uncle kamum." Like hell, yeah I wanted to know more about her, thank you. Is she seeing anyone, I'd like to know that too. The conductor beckoned at me for our fare as stranger mother shoved stranger baby crazy into my face to retrieve her purse. And damn no lil' biih. This guy right here isn't your "uncle" I gave the conductor 200bob and he asked me "wangapi?". I said mmoja like a boss and he gave me 100bob and motioned at the lady to hand me the 50bob she had. The baby grabbed my now 50 shilling note and said, "Leta pesa ya mamangu." Her mother looked at her adoringly and smiled. I wondered what was going on in her head.(aaaww...I'm bringing up a thief who's right about now being seriously flogged in someone's mind) Beep...Beep...umefika wapi? It was Bae. Oh no! Remember I was "in Nyayo" 30 minutes ago, right? I was in Nyayo for reals now. I didn't know whether to text her tumepotea njia or what. And oh was there traffic in Nyayo!! I decided to alight and walk up past the last round about to tao where traffic is normally fluid. Baby crazy  was asleep by now. "Excuse me" I pleaded. "Unashuka?" Me:"Yeah" Inner me:"Hapanaa, naenda washrooms huko nyuma ya basi." She tilted her laps sideways as I squeezed my way out from my seat. I pulled my  fifty-shilling note from the surprisingly firm grip and she immediately woke up in a mouthful of wails. Cry or cry not, if they thought I was going to pay their fare after the pride I'd invested in saying Mmoja, they had clearly over-estimated my social status with reference to my fashion taste. I stepped off the bus in a virtual slow motion as the crying baby's noises made for the background music. The breeze blew into my face as I took in a luñgful of cool, fresh air, finally free of imposed responsibilities. I turned to make a face at the girl but my phone buzzed midway.  Oh dammit!! I have a date to get to and a lie to come up with!! Run brother, run!

content: Name: John Doe No.: A35797 Date: 31/12/1999 Time:11:50 p.m Mode Of Death: Electrocution I have always been a lover of words. Words have been the only thing people never succeeded in taking away from me and they are powerful in the right hands. Yet I do not intend to be pious and say that my hands have never shook at the gravity they hold. When enclosed between these daunting walls you think too much. You have way too much time on your hands to think about your sins and the endless thread of guilt it strings along. My sins have purged and purged my soul till I am just a frame of the man I used to be. Jail must be the prequel to hell. We are allowed one letter before we die. Maybe I would have written about love if I had been kind enough to offer myself that, but that wasn't my lot in life. I don't complain though, life is a balanced diet, most of us just hate the vegetables. I don't really know what to write in my last letter. How funny! I've always been a man of many words but sometimes my pen bleeds and wrings its own soul from itself upon touching the paper. But sometimes it echoes the sound of its own silence. It's eerie, lifeless. See I'm not a consistent creator. I create a world and walk out from it, leave the content to wander in search of its own purpose. Maybe they're damned, but I fail to see how that's different from any of us. I've always been a hard headed kid. One of the many reasons that got me here I presume. I remember my mom's mantra, "A hard head makes a soft butt." Maybe that's why she thrashed me with fire in her eyes. Only it bugged me that she seemed to enjoy it. Her demons always came out to play whenever she laid her hand on me. As a kid I never got away with anything because I could never bring my tongue to fold over backwards when required to tell a lie. Nevertheless, I never relent pursuing anything I set my heart out to do and that attitude is the right temperature for trouble to brew in this city. A lot of things are twisted in this city. It's a city full of undertones of sin, and death has learnt to crawl stealthily as it lurks in the shadows. In this city there are no heroes, no one can afford to go against the system. This city is a wilderness of sorts only that there is no forbidden fruit. Nothing grows here because nothing touches the light and lives to see it. It's a city heavily stratified into social classes the very heart of it is wrapped in chaos. This city is toxic, it ruins anything it touches. It's like the first woman you loved, the woman who took away all your innocence, found out what your desires were, made you thirsty for them and left you high and dry tangled in the veins of your lust. You should never once think she even loved you. More than anything I've always wanted to clean up this city. Salvage whatever is left of my home. But when you live in this city long enough you learn that you can't clean up the system from the inside. Once you get into the system it corrupts you, it taints you leaving you with nothing but a charred soul. It's a city formed by its highlights and shadows never midtones. However the good thing about this place is everyone fights for something. There is nothing as deadly as a man who wants nothing. Some fight for money, revenge, others just love the thrill; these are often the ones who die fast. But very few people are afraid of death anyway. I guess when you see too many of those around you die it becomes the norm. You get used to the cold embrace of death and it surprises you how comfortable its arms can be.  I never really knew what death would be like for me though. I remember fearing it but when they killed my friend, the stars shone so bright and the moon smiled at the world, I couldn't help but think it a sweet relief. People are here today and gone in a whiff. The thing about dying men is that we're brutally honest so I wish I had a chance to talk to the younger me right now. Maybe it would have changed how things will end. I'd tell him to make his life count for we're all doomed anyway. Not to just believe in something but to stand up for it and die from it if it kills him because maybe dreams and passions makes us suicidal that way. I'd tell him to never fear opening the door when love knocks. To love deeply enough to lay himself bare in someone's hands saying here take every piece of me to get closer to you. To love till he burns in his own desire. I'd tell him to fight for those he loves. I'd tell him to laugh a lot more. I never did laugh quite enough. Maybe I wish I had another shot at life. To get released into the world and grow old with a woman by my side. Adopt some children and have them hold my hand as I face death. But I can't. Someone somewhere much higher than me has decided that I am toxic to this city. Someone somewhere much higher than me decided that I should be identified by a number. Someone somewhere much higher than me has decided that I have to die by electricity. I always knew studying it in university would pay off! Kind regards, A dead man.

content: Celebrants have always observed numerous well known and lesser-known New Year practices and superstitions. All of them rooting around the belief that if you engage in an event at the beggining of the year, it will set the pattern for the year to come. Therefore, when I received a hot slap with majestic capriciousness from my old man at the beggining of 2016, I was prepared for my cheeks to have unceremonious caresses and massage throught the year. That might explain why I am not much of a high-five enthusiast. I can not well predict the intentions of a high raised open palm. Beleifs: Scaring the shit outta people since the days of Sakawa the Great. Buckle up and lets take a ride to Kisii, shall we? The folkore surrounding black cats varying from culture to culture, I was surprised to read that the Scot believe that a black cat is prosperity. Haha. Ha. Hah. No no. No way. The black cat and a Kisii witch work together. Always. Like Waiguru and NYS. You cant mention one without thinking the other. And this one I have come to beleive it's true myself. What is that you mumbled? Huh? Ooh yeah. I know. All these years of education and I haven't shaken that stupid thinking. But let me tell you, I cant see a black cat on our roof and you tell me it's going where? Shopping? To see friends? A meeting? Yeah. A meeting maybe. With it's witch master. You see there is a story told of this mama who had the blackest cat on black Kisii. She also had a son in the States. The son invited the mama to States to stay with him. So when the son went to night shifts, he left the mother alone in the house. Na si you know how zunyes have cameras everywhere. So this weekend the son is on leave and the neighbor comes to him. " We want your mother to be our trainer." " What do you mean trainor?" Mogaka, with a puzzled look asks. " You know, the person who teaches another pers... " " I know the meaning of trainor, Mr Smith! What skill do you think my mother has that she should train you? " Mr Smith goes on to show Mogaka a clip from his cctv footage. " You see, your mother can run really well. she works out at midnight, when the world is silent. Look at that speed, very energetic, very acrobatic" That's the story of how the mama came back home. " Is it not the other day I was sucking mucus out of your nostrils? " She lamented as she was being driven to the airport. " Yes, I am glad you did that. But I dont want anymore embarrassments of you running around at night naked. Go practice that back home. And besides, I can now buy a handkerchief so I need no nostril-sucking festivals." * Drum rolls* duru du dudu du du!-Enters the Praying mantis (I used these somewhere and someone thought it was cool. Haha. I hope it is.) So, the praying mantis. If you have seen me you have already seen a praying mantis. Okay, body structure of course. Big head, skinny body and an eerily adult face. I have grown up to both respect and  fear this insect. Not because we look alike but because of the belief sorrounding it's existence. I ussualy think I am more of a black scorpion myself by the way. But I digress. We are told in our land that if you see a praying mantis  prepare your toothpicks, meat is on the way. I envied the power this insect held. The power that came with knowing whether a group of people would eat meat or not. So when I was unapologetically served hot cabbage one day after a mantis sight-seeing, I hated the ancestors for lying. They had betrayed my trust . And that is how I became skeptic of most things I heard. But if you come to think of it, some are utterly lies. Debauchery by elders. Ati only the men are allowed to eat the back of a chicken and the gizzard. That ancestors will curse women who ate these two parts. If the man of the house is not there, the eldest son does the honors. This I believe was a scheme by the men to just eat a lot. So when one day my old man failed to make an appearance after we cooked chicken, I started early celebration.  I was singing. "Bruk off ya back" was the song on my mind allthrough. And then at the last minute when I had already called enzymes for a convocation in my mouth, an uncle showed up. Very comfortably sitting down and mama insisted he eats supper. Man! Was I livid. So that chance was gone. "Omoigwa are you fine? " " Yes, yes ofcourse tata moke, why wouldn't I be?" I fake a smile. I curse him as his chocolate teeth sink into the chicken back. I mutter something under my breath to the effect of him having a bout of diarrhoea and rashes on his buttocks the whole week. Then there is firewood. Ati there is this stone cave in a hill at my place. That when you pass there you have to throw firewood at this cave and blessings will come your way. Methinks issa lie. Yaani some old papas somewhere bila wives created this so that when you throw the firewood there they bring their lazy old selfs and take it home. Then they would light a small fire at the cave to make you believe the next morning ati spirits had discussions there about you. SMH. ( Shaking my head while waking away with all the firewood I can gather) But others I do think are true. When bees settle in your house then wealth and prosperity is coming your way. Just check, whoever you will find has bees making a beehive inside their houses, trust me they have a prosperous family. But other beleifs were just childish, llike when you are able to step on the shadow of an eagle you will get two hundred shillings. I haven't still managed to step on one. But if I break off from your company like a madman and chase after a shadow, please understand where I come from. What do you beleive in?

content: My buttocks vibrate. Wizkid shouts.I thrust my hand back there and yank my phone out. It's one of our great fans calling to ask about Friday. "Yo! Maze what happened to Friday? " "He got too loud at creation and was placed at the backrow of days of the week by God." I respond. "No, I mean the usual thing you guys do. The.... The Child of Africa or something like that. " He persists. Obviously he didn't get my funny reply and neither does he the name of our blog. "Yeeeeeaaah, about that. " I mumble having realised I hadn't prepared for this grilling. "There have been technical issues with the editing crew so we didn't make it. But we shall sure correct." I lied. Through my two big front teeth. When I zoned out on Friday while waiting for my guy who was supposed to do the Friday piece, I woke up later to realise he actually hadn't. So I slept and brushed it off ati maybe he had network issues and would post in the morning. Or maybe he had stepped on a knife while walking around in his bedsitter. You can never be sure of this bedsitters. You walk in tip toeing like it's a landmine just incase you step on a hot pan you had carelessly placed. Or a knife as I assumed with my homeboy. I woke up on Friday. He hadn't posted. My inbox was full of disgruntled readers wanting to know why. I also didn't. So I decide to call my guy to know what's popping on his end. Maybe he had been invited at a barbeque and couldn't post.Heck, maybe he was the main spit at the barbeque and cant write while being grilled. He can't handle two commitments. But seriously, he has never had the ability to traverse various ends of the social spectrum. You guys know TDH right? Tall, Dark and Handsome for those who went to Mukubu School for African Sons and Gentlemen. Now my guy is Tall and Dark. The H is missing. He is not that pleasant to the eye to be frank. He is 18 but he looks 40. But he has always has a fine damsel at his side. It pains me especially when this guy with with his look, a seething disposition of the face of a chameleon if you ask me, can get fine ghels easily. So at some point in my assumptions I think  he had decided to seek the wise counsel of a plastic surgeon. So I call him up. Druuuuuu. Click. "Brotherman!! " He shouts. Sidenote. ( Let it be known I hate being called this by people visiting plastic surgeons). "Msee, where are you? " I question. I hear a buzzle of activities in the background. Motorbikes mostly. Motor- bikes huh? Kisumu. Bungoma. Githurai. Coast............ India. Aaaaah India. Yep. Yep. Plastic surgery!. " Usitense, I am working on something and need to be away from humans kiasi" He says. "Yeah, so much from someone who doesn't look like a human being himself." No. I didn't tell him that though. So we chat a bit as my FBI brain tries to decipher where he is. He refuses to let open his co-ordinates and says I am even very lucky to find him on phone. I wish him good luck and all. End of call. "Damn!" I curse. Sijamuuliza kuhusu post ya Friday. So I call him back. Number temporarily out of service. Yep! Looks like he was right. I was lucky to have found him on call. For someone whose face doesn't look right, he is right for once. What would Magufuli do? Write a post for him? So I go through the trailer he gave on Tuesday. Politics. Mehn, I don't know how to write about politics.....yet. What will I write? How some guy was slapped in 2007 at my hometown for mentioning Kibaki? He had been warned though. He was told that if as small as his fart smelled of Kibaki's name he would see. Or would I write about that guy whose onions were urinated on and farm full of sugarcane looted because he was a Raila fan and others weren't? But no. My stories aren't what my guy might want to write. He is a serious person,accompanied with a serious face( pun totally intended). Maybe he wants to write why we should or should not vote. Maybe he wants to say how we only rant behind our keyboards about change and still retreat back to our tribal shells at the polling station. I would never tell though what he wanted to write. So until he is found, untill he resurfaces from Githurai or India or wherever in the name of Pubungu pakwach he is, I can't guess. So his will be the post that never was. PS. He will write about it though when he comes. You will even tell by the look on his face.

content: Everyone writes about love. Writers craft their emotions into lines and proses that seek to draw the objects of their affection. They are ready to cut themselves open so their love would pour out and in a fire, consume them both. But no one ever writes about being the object of that affection. Knowing that you have the power to break the core of one's humanity. That you wear the face of Cupid and Thanatos-god of death. That you can tear apart towers so beautifully built of poetry and music. That you can kill such poor things without their resistance. No one ever writes that love can be a demon if it wills. Yet I wish I could tell you about my exes. But they are just Cinderellas whose feet never fit in the glass shoes. Caught in the crossfire between the two warring parties my possesiveness and love are. I've left wounds that are unforgivable. Wounds only mother's are supposed to make for they know they will be there to play nurse. Her name was Nina. The only trophy in my collection of lost lovers I am not proud of because I was not the one she loved most. She particularly liked the foggy nights. The ones in which the air hung heavily and draped its shoulders as if gasping for the very same breath, still and pregnant with anticipation. She loved it when the fog concealed, albeit momentarily, who she had chosen to be. A night that the clouds crowd so protectively around the moon as if shielding it from the nonchalant stares of us humans, so effortlessly oblivious of its presence. A night full of so much energy yet exhibiting so little. On such nights, when even the coldest heart would ache for some warmth even though it would mean thawing. Yet change, even the slightest is uncomfortable. She marvelled at how the strong shook at the soft whispers of the wind, the tumbling finality of it all. She stood by the lone lampost on 5th street. Propping her weight on one leg and raising the other to touch the lampost to form a perfect four. She hated her bony knees. The neon light fell gracefully on her in a way that it caressed her face and cupped it so gently in its arms unlike most men she knew who would fall for her head first, me included. Her high cheekbones, silky hair flowed so smoothly without even trying( unlike those of her counterparts that seemed to defy all the laws of gravity and stand up on their two feet as if in protest ) She chuckled at the thought. All she needed now was one more round and she would have enough for her completion. Just one round. Snapping her out of thought was a car that halted right at her feet. He knew the drill, and so did she. In cities like this, on nights like this, in places like this where you could read the raging lust off peoples faces, how could they not? But she had learnt to be numb. How to never get her feelings intertwined. And as he went on with his guilty pleasure, she couldn't help but stare emptily at the ceiling trying to whisk this moment away. She drowned out his moans in the sea of her flooding thoughts. She closed her eyes and waited for the weight to lift from above her. She was like a cardboard that too many nails had fit in and pulled out, leaving her with too many holes. Standing at my door, she held a note in her hand and handed it over to me. It was as if I held her life force and it pained me to see her that way. So high and dry for a drug that tasted sweet with her since mine were not the arms that calmed her demons. I took the note as one fully aware of my vantage point in her life. I thought of the note, crisp, almost feels holy yet for such an evil thing, the paradox in it all. I chuckled at my own wit as I pressed a puny sized sachet half filled with a white powder into her palm. Her completion. I watched her walk to her shack, a walk punctuated by fits of running. She felt desperately the need to be complete. The need to be  in the arms that gathered her together. She like many other people was not really looking for the meaning of life but for the experience of being alive. Lost in a world that moves too fast, like one in a maze, concepts of life too subtle to grasp and too vain to clasp. I imaginied as she sat on her couch, the same couch that I kissed her on, she burnt the powder, held her pipe and took it all in. She watched as the perplexities and worries of life merge into one sheet of complete nothingness. Nothing meant anything and she loved that. She passed out, only to resuscitate the morning after. Nina. Some people ask me if it pained me to see a woman I love fated to be sedated in the claws of a drug. What they don't know is that she was the epitome of beauty and I understood that I was not the only one who drank from her well. My memory of her is a jigsaw puzzle of my absence, her hurt and ache I could never heal. There's no greater hurt than watching someone you love, love their drugs more than you. I can never fit the pieces of her into each other. That's why I can't let myself love another again. It's sin alright, but the devil can't be talked out of his trade. I am a collector of hearts just not this one.

content: Annus horribilis: - That is the best term to describe last year. 2016 was messed up for most of us, yes? We lost soldiers in Somalia, we got mad when we discovered that SGR trains look like old tractors, 5 billion from the health ministry went missing, teargas Monday destroyed businesses and if you were in form four last year, CS Matiang’i made sure you didn’t get an A. Worse still, when we thought the problems just existed in Kenya; Donald Trump was declared president elect; simply annus horrribilis. Even Kofi Olomide can tell you the year was horrible… but I digress, this post is not about all that. Today we’ll talk about the problems no one talks about; nothing generic,nothing newsworthy or even blog worthy. Today we open up and talk about the E that stared at you with a menacing look last sem, the rejections that came your way, the horrible relationship you developed with your dad. The audition that you rehearsed over and over again and did not work out, the interview that you slouched. Just for this Friday, allow me to remind you of the heart break you got last year.Let us open up to the first sexual encounter that you thought would be great and well you just realized it was a booty call, yeah? Class 7s- Mathare Primary School. I presumably carried some of these frustrations last year when I became a teacher at Mathare slums. Then I started noticing something weird. I had an English lesson with class 7s every Wednesday at 3:30pm. I would try to make them write great compositions, but of course it was tough. These are slum kids and English is almost foreign to them. To easen up the task, I made them narrate to me the stories they wanted to write and slowly I would teach them how to construct meaningful paragraphs. But pretty much, these kids taught me more about life than I could ever teach them. They told me dismaying stories of how they are forced into child trafficking and into selling drugs. The girls told me how they were introduced to prostitution by their own mothers, they told me how they slept hungry or how their father came home drunk and forced them to sleep outside. Sadly, they even told me of how their boyfriends bought them red undies on valentines and later forced their way through them.These kids told me stories that I felt were of disgrace and later those kids wrote the best stories on drug and substance abuse, they wrote the most beautiful stories on talent, on overcoming fear, about their dreams. None of those kids was sad, they were always jovial and looked forward to Wednesday lessons and so did I.Those kids have tenacity and the will to live. They know what life truly is and they live it; whether it makes them happy or not. They are so used to their sad stories that they are no longer sad; they are stories they want to tell when they grow up. Vulnerability. You see the thing with our problems is that we do not talk about them. We do not talk because we don’t want anyone regarding us as weak; we fear being thought of as weak.  We don’t talk because we don’t want to be a burden to others. We don’t want anyone to think we cannot make it, so we put on this tough façade. And what do we do after that? We become better people? No. I’ll tell you what we do; we build walls and drown ourselves in depression, we drink ourselves silly till we are wretched souls, we bake weed all day and get lit and then you know what next? We become comfortable with that life. You disagree? Then tell me why is it that when you ask someone of their love story; they end up telling you a tale of a horrifying heartbreak? Why is it that the stories of connection end up being stories of disconnect? Yet, at the same time, why do a million stories of success have a bearing on suffering? Maybe; that single entity that defines someone as weak is actually the only other way to define them as strong. Maybe, that thin line between weakness and strength is being vulnerable to our own stories. Perhaps like Steve Jobs, remembering that we'll be dead soon is the most important tool we need to make the big choices in life. Because almost everything - all external expectations, all pride, all fear of embarrassment or failure - these things just fall away in the face of death, leaving only what is truly important. Am I saying you should go telling everyone about your problems? By all means, no.I am just saying the next time someone asks you if you are fine and you really are not, tell them. The next time someone is willing to listen to you; do not push them away.The next time; just before you conclude that nothing ever works out in life ,reach out. You might just be surprised at how much similar crap we all go through and maybe just maybe the only difference is how we turn out. Owning our stories can be hard but not nearly as difficult as spending our lives running from it. Embracing our vulnerabilities is risky but not nearly as dangerous as giving up on love and belonging and joy—the experiences that make us the most vulnerable. Only when we are brave enough to explore the darkness will we discover the infinite power of our light. ~Brené Brown

content: I knew a buddy back in my previous life, High School you'd call it. For security purposes we shall call this guy Evans, because that is actually his real name. Now, young me fresh from the Kingdom of bananas had minute problems in conversation. I would end my conversations with the term, "aajee?!" of course in the obvious rising Kisii intonation. It would come out as an exclamation and a question at the same time. Call it the mother paradox of all intonations. So people would stay there waiting for me to either signal them for an expected answer, or continue with my mostly mundane folkores. Nikamwambia aajee?!!.......nikamtusi aajee?!! Nikagusa huyo msichana halafu akageuka aajee?!!... Evans is the one who made me realise I had this intonation. He would tease me everytime. Which mostly pissed me off. But then I would tell him to pronounce the word contented. Hailing from the vast Eastern region of Kenya, the guy would bite his tongue off to small pieces before he finished the damn word. He would say condended. I swear by Sakawa the Great,that used to make me happy. Then I would forget my aajee?! and say it sometimes. And we start all over again. You see, the kids in my highschool couldn't give you peace if the smallest accent was traced in your conversation. There were small grammar cartels,probably distant relatives of Shakespeare. So dudes of our likes straight from shagz would only talk to ourselves and in hushed tones.I swear I'm not lying. They even started a notice board at the back of the class permanently with a list of  people who mixed up matamchi yao. Hell, someone even had a notebook specifically for this stuff. You are there in class eating story then you say you were in a baraimary( primary) school somewhere in Mandera. The whole class goes oooooooooh and the cartel is chewing your buttocks off. Unawekwa bahari yake ,aye? Mundane stuff I'm telling you. But it got serious .This was outright bullying. Shagz dudes we had to adopt or lose this pronunciation battles. So we retreated to ourselves and organised tribal groups where no one notices that umeng'oa because they either don'know or are also busy mispronouncing their own words. These in turn became the bookworms who would top class since time for conversations was spent on books. I'm not saying I was one of them, but I'm not saying I wasn't . Yaani ninasema aajee?!..heck, forget it. I don't know if they amuse or amaze me, but my tribesmen are a funny crew. If you know one of them you've probably heard the word gaki in their sentences. They just throw the Kisii terms in there aimlessly like flying faeces in a paper bag. And they are very brave. They don't give a rat's ass If you notice their matamshi. Terrorists of language. Very brave they should be all working for KDF and plundering English and Swahili and the border, away from the serene of hardworking Kenyans.I have been a client of this conversation slaughtering. My word being ,obe! , an exclaimer mostly, It was curtailed by highschool grammar cartels. But I eventually joined the Nazis  after perfecting my own grammar. They say if you cant beat the donkeys, join them. I don't  know who they are. So having finally gone to the big city and read books, I knew my home crush would finally fall for me. So I did what every Mogaka doesn't do to a Moraa, I approached her. We hit the inner murram road with the sugarcane I had bought her. The inner road is where village women can't see us easily. They are very mouthy and might cook up stories. And also to avoid the local boys who might beat me because they think I am finishing for them options for girls. Ha. Fancy that. They will just beat me in front of my crush. Ugly nutheads. Anyway, She was there biting that sugarcane very sexily and that really gets to me. It is a mini date this one. Me in a date with my crush. Even if I die now, I have made it in life. Then the way she chewed the juice out of it melted my heart. I was in love. Hah. Me in love. Even my mother would laugh at that. Good thing she can't read this post. So we are there and she has her white handkerchief and bata rubber shoes. I don't know what it is with Kisii chics and white handkerchiefs on their hands. So anyway again, we are walking and I am talking and I pause from time to time allowing her to bite my picnic sugarcane. I have invested in her. Today I might get a tumble. The dryspell I have is climate change I tell you; global warming.I am there throwing a few Nairobi sheng jargons while sprinkling a bit Kisii not to lose her totally. She does smile. "Where do you go to church?" I ask her as I try to rack my brains for a better vibe. As I wait for her reply I am thinking of my next lines. These flirts don't lay themselves son. "Sent Balls Catholic." "St. where now???" With a puzzled look . Few things rarely puzzle me. I am a man of constant face. "St. Balls, kwani husikii poa?" She replies. She doesn't use my rare puzzled face as clue that I want to hear Paul's name in her sentence, not her weird fetish church. I put my face back in the chambers. I had wasted it. Then I break into laughter and she doesn't like it. I can't explain to her because she thinks she is right. And women are always right. So  we argued and I lost her. All I want to say bottomline is simple here, since I can't go on writing forever. It doesn't matter how you pronounce words in English or Swahili as long as you know you can write them well and can still with your accent make yourself well understood. Whether you lace them with some local condend like my Kamba friend or say pananas like my folks, just live on. Kalembe Ndile has apparently studied abroad but you'd think he went to Kithakaine Polytechnic .But again we should laugh at each other. It is healthy as long as we don't do it too much,because some of the pronunciations are utterly funny. Like a Somali friend of mine who will write a boem some day for us.
